items.title: Major fever criterion
items.contentHtml:
Fever ≥5 days and four criteria

  • The diagnosis of Kawasaki disease requires fever ≥5 days (without another explanation) plus at least four of the five main clinical criteria.
  • If the fever persists ≥5 days but there are fewer than four criteria, Kawasaki incomplete should be suspected and complementary evaluation should be performed (echocardiogram, biomarkers).
  • Fever is usually >39 °C, remitting, and does not respond to conventional antipyretics; Its duration is the diagnostic pillar.

items.title: Bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis
items.contentHtml:
Bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis

  • Bilateral conjunctivitis without exudate, painless, sparing the limbus (non-purulent conjunctival injection).
  • It is one of the five main clinical criteria; appears in ~90% of cases.
  • Key differential diagnosis: adenovirus (exudate, pharyngoconjunctival fever) and measles (Koplik spots).

items.title: Polymorphous rash
items.contentHtml:
Polymorphic rash

  • Polymorphic rash: morbilliform, scarlatiniform or urticariform; It appears on the trunk and extremities, without vesicles or scabs.
  • It does not affect the palms or soles (unlike hand, foot, and mouth disease).
  • It must be differentiated from viral exanthems (rubella, parvovirus B19) and Still's disease.

items.title: Changes in extremities
items.contentHtml:
Changes in extremities

  • Acute phase: palmo-plantar erythema and indurated edema of the hands and feet (“red hands” sign).
  • Subacute phase (2-3 weeks): characteristic periungual peeling, which begins in the foot pads and can extend.
  • These findings are highly specific and are part of the main clinical criteria.

items.title: Oral disorders
items.contentHtml:
Oral alterations

  • Raspberry tongue (red hypertrophic papillae) and diffuse erythema of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa.
  • Dry, cracked, fissured and erythematous lips; There may be lip bleeding.
  • There are no oral ulcers or exudate (different from herpes simplex or streptococcal pharyngitis).

items.title: Cervical adenopathy
items.contentHtml:
Cervical adenopathy

  • Unilateral cervical adenopathy >1.5 cm in diameter, non-suppurative, of acute onset.
  • It is the least common criterion (~50% of cases), but the most specific when present.
  • Differential diagnosis: bacterial adenitis (suppuration, response to antibiotics) and cervical tuberculosis.

items.foco: The most serious complication: coronary aneurysms
items.title: Coronary aneurysms
items.contentHtml:
Coronary aneurysms

  • The most serious complication is the development of coronary aneurysms, which occur in 25% of untreated patients.
  • The risk is reduced to <5% if intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg single dose is administered within the first 10 days.
  • Echocardiogram is the standard for detecting dilation. or aneurysms (Z score >2.5 suggests abnormality).

items.foco: Initial treatment: IV immunoglobulin and aspirin
items.title: Initial treatment
items.contentHtml:
Initial treatment: intravenous immunoglobulin and aspirin

  • First-line treatment combines intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg single dose plus aspirin in anti-inflammatory doses (30-50 mg/kg/day)..
  • Intravenous immunoglobulin reduces arterial inflammation and the risk of coronary aneurysms.
  • Aspirin is administered initially in anti-inflammatory doses until the fever subsides, then it is reduced to antiplatelet doses (3-5 mg/kg/day) if there are no coronary alterations.

items.title: Intravenous immunoglobulin dosage
items.contentHtml:
Dose of intravenous immunoglobulin: 2 g/kg single dose

  • Intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg single dose is the standard regimen, administered as a 10-12 hour infusion.
  • If the fever persists >36 hours after the infusion, it is considered Kawasaki resistant to intravenous immunoglobulin; second dose or methylprednisolone (30 mg/kg/day) may be used.
  • Do not use intravenous immunoglobulin after 10 days of fever if the patient is afebrile, unless there is evidence of active inflammation (evolving aneurysms).

items.title: Anti-inflammatory aspirin dosage
items.contentHtml:
Anti-inflammatory dose of aspirin: 30-50 mg/kg/day

  • Aspirin in anti-inflammatory dose (30-50 mg/kg/day) divided into four doses during the acute phase, until 48-72 hours after defervescence.
  • Then it is reduced to aspirin in anti-aggregant doses (3-5 mg/kg/day) in a single dose if the echocardiogram is normal.
  • Risk of Reye's syndrome: avoid aspirin in viral infections (chickenpox, influenza) if antiplatelet dosage is maintained; consider alternatives (clopidogrel).

items.foco: Incomplete Kawasaki: suspect with fever and fewer criteria
items.title: incomplete Kawasaki
items.contentHtml:
Incomplete Kawasaki: suspect with fever and fewer criteria

  • It is defined as fever ≥5 days plus <4 clinical criteria, but with suggestive laboratory or echocardiographic findings.
  • Laboratory indicators: C-reactive protein >3 mg/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate >40 mm/h, hypoalbuminemia, anemia, thrombocytosis (>450,000/mm³) in the subacute phase.
  • If suspected, perform echocardiogram; if coronary aneurysms or Z score >2.5 are detected, treat as full Kawasaki.

items.foco: Control echocardiogram at diagnosis and follow-up
items.title: Echocardiogram and follow-up
items.contentHtml:
Control echocardiogram at diagnosis and follow-up

  • Echocardiogram at baseline at diagnosis (ideally within the first 7 days) and at 2-4 weeks (subacute phase).
  • Evaluate coronary diameters (Z score), presence of aneurysms, thrombi, myocardial function and pericardial effusion.
  • Long-term follow-up: if present aneurysms, repeat echocardiogram after 6 months and annually; If there are no lesions, after 6-12 months and then every 3-5 years.
